1: Chapter 15 opens with a new divine speech unit addressed to Moses after the sentence of Numbers 14. The placement is theologically significant: immediately after pronouncing the wilderness-generation sentence, YHWH begins legislating for the land. The offering laws of Numbers 15 presuppose entry into Canaan (kî tāḇōʾû ʾel-ʾereṣ), assuring the next generation that the promise remains in force even as the current generation is excluded.
2: ʾel-bənê yiśrāʾēl — to the Israelites — the address covers all of Israel, not the doomed generation alone. The legislation is future-oriented: ʾăšer ʾănî nōtēn lākem — which I am giving you. The present participle marks the land promise as ongoing and certain despite the immediately preceding judgment. This structural juxtaposition is the canonical statement: judgment on one generation does not annul YHWH's covenant purpose.
3: The אִשֶּׁה (ʾiššeh) — food offering by fire — is the generic category covering burnt offerings, vow fulfillments, freewill offerings, and festival offerings. The phrase ləʿăśôt rêaḥ nîḥôaḥ laYHWH — to make a pleasing aroma to YHWH — uses the olfactory idiom that appears throughout Leviticus; it expresses acceptance, the offering achieving its intended purpose of divine reception.
4: The graduated grain and oil quantities create a proportional system: smaller animals require smaller accompaniments, larger animals require larger ones. עֲשִׂירִת הָאֵפָה (ʿăśîrît hāʾēpâ) — one-tenth of an ephah — approximately two liters of fine flour. הִין (hîn) is an Egyptian-derived liquid measure. The precision of these specifications serves cultic integrity: the offering must meet defined requirements, not be ad hoc.
5: יַיִן (yayin) — wine — as drink offering. A quarter hîn accompanies lamb-size offerings. The drink offering (nesek) complements the grain offering and burnt offering, completing a meal-like presentation: bread (grain), oil (fat), and wine (drink). The tripartite offering structure reflects the full range of agricultural produce, offering back to YHWH the firstfruits of what the land will yield.
6: For a ram (larger animal), the quantities double: two-tenths of an ephah of flour and one-third of a hin of oil. The graduated scale distinguishes between categories of animals in terms of both their value and the proportional offering they require. The system ensures that larger offerings carry larger obligations, preventing the reduction of significant sacrifices to minimal accompaniments.
7: One-third of a hin of wine for the ram's drink offering. The repetition of rêaḥ nîḥôaḥ laYHWH — a pleasing aroma to YHWH — across the graduated offerings asserts that each tier achieves the same acceptance regardless of size; the smaller offering is equally valid.
8: The bull (ben-bāqār) — the largest common sacrifice — carries the highest requirements. The bull appears in peace/fellowship offerings, vow fulfillment, and burnt offerings. The specification covers all major sacrificial contexts for the largest animal category, ensuring the accompanying offerings scale appropriately.
9: Three-tenths of an ephah and half a hin of oil for the bull — the quantities have increased from the lamb's one-tenth/one-quarter to the bull's three-tenths/half hin. The graduated scale demonstrates a consistent proportional logic: the more valuable the sacrifice, the more substantial the grain-and-oil presentation that accompanies it.
10: Half a hin of wine accompanies the bull, completing the graduated scale. The phrase ʾiššeh rêaḥ nîḥôaḥ laYHWH — a food offering, a pleasing aroma to YHWH — closes each tier with identical acceptance language, affirming that the whole system — from the smallest lamb to the largest bull — falls within the same framework of divine reception.
11: kākâ yēʿāśeh lāšôr hāʾeḥāḏ ʾô lāʾayil hāʾeḥāḏ — this shall be done for each bull or ram. The legal rubric extends the graduated system to all animals without exception. The formula language — kākâ yēʿāśeh (thus shall it be done) — appears throughout sacrificial law as a standardizing closure marking the universal application of what has been stipulated.
12: Whatever the number of animals, the same proportional system applies per animal. The principle is per-animal consistency: the total offering scales linearly with the number of animals, preventing either inflation or reduction of the required accompaniments based on quantity.
13: Every ʾezrāḥ — native-born/citizen — shall follow this procedure. The word אֶזְרָח (ʾezrāḥ) literally means one who sprouts or rises from the soil, suggesting indigenous rootedness. It stands in contrast to gēr (resident alien), which the following verses include equally. The primary audience is the settled Israelite community, but the law's inclusivity in vv14-16 extends beyond it.
14: The resident alien (גֵּר, gēr) — a sojourner without full tribal citizenship who lives under Israel's protection — is included under the same law. The inclusion of the gēr in the sacrificial system is characteristic of P legislation and reflects the covenant community's openness to non-ethnic members who live within its framework. bəkol-dōrōtêkem — throughout your generations — makes the inclusion permanent.
15: ḥuqqâ ʾaḥat — one statute — governing both native and alien. The word חֻקָּה (ḥuqqâ) — statute/ordinance — marks this as binding law rather than custom. The inclusion clause kāgēr kāʾezrāḥ yihyeh — as the resident alien so the native — formally equalizes the legal status of both categories before YHWH. The social distinction remains; the cultic standing is identical.
16: tôrâ ʾaḥat ûmišpāṭ ʾeḥāḏ yihyeh lākem — one law and one ordinance for you. תּוֹרָה (tôrâ) (instruction/law) and מִשְׁפָּט (mišpāṭ) (judgment/ordinance) together form a comprehensive legal pair. The verse is remarkable in the ancient Near Eastern context: most legal systems maintained strict distinctions between citizens and resident aliens. Israel's sacrificial law intentionally equalizes them, anticipating the universalism more fully developed in the prophets (Isaiah 56:6-7).
17: A new divine speech unit legislating the תְּרוּמָה (tərûmâ) — contribution/heave offering — of the first dough. The repeated speech formula (waydabber YHWH ʾel-Moshe lēmôr) signals a new discrete legal unit, not a continuation of the offering quantities. The laws of vv17-21 address a different aspect of the offering system: the gift from the first-processed grain of the harvest.
18: bəḇōʾăkem ʾel-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer ʾănî mēḇîʾ ʾetkem šāmmâ — when you come into the land to which I am bringing you. The syntax emphasizes YHWH as the active bringer (mēḇîʾ); Israel's entry is not their own achievement but divine leading. This framing continues the land-promise assurance that follows the judgment of Numbers 14 — the land remains the destination YHWH is leading to.
19: When eating from the land's produce (ûmilleḥem hāʾāreṣ tōʾkelû), Israel shall present a contribution (tərûmâ) to YHWH. The gift from the agricultural yield frames every harvest meal as an act of acknowledgment: what grows from the land belongs to YHWH. The offering transforms ordinary eating into covenantal reception of divine gift.
20: The specific offering is a loaf of ʿărîsôt — the first of the dough, from the threshing-floor gift (kirûmat gōren). עָרִיסָה (ʿărîsâ) appears to mean a lump of dough; the offering is made before baking commences, giving YHWH the first processed grain. The comparison kəterûmat gōren — as the contribution from the threshing floor — connects bread-making to harvest offering as parallel first-fruits gestures.
21: mērēʾšît ʿărîsōtêkem tittənû laYHWH tərûmâ — from the first of your dough you shall give a contribution to YHWH. The phrase lədōrōtêkem — throughout your generations — frames the law as permanent agricultural practice, not one-time inaugural offering. The first-of-dough gift becomes a recurring covenant act embedded in the routine of Israelite domestic life, connecting kitchen and sanctuary.
22: The šegāgâ legislation begins (vv22-29): laws governing unintentional sin by the community. שְׁגָגָה (šəgāgâ) — inadvertent error, stumbling/wandering from the right path — is distinguished from intentional transgression throughout Leviticus (Numbers 4:2, 13; Numbers 5:18) and Numbers. The root šāgag means to go astray, to err. The legal distinction between inadvertent and deliberate sin is fundamental to the atonement system.
23: The scope: all commandments YHWH commanded through Moses, from the day of the commandment onwards throughout generations. The formula emphasizes the continuous applicability of the Mosaic legislation: there is no statute of limitations on covenantal obligation. The phrase ûlahāʾlāh lədōrōtêkem — and onward throughout your generations — frames the law as perpetually binding.
24: Community unintentional sin (min-hāʿēḏâ bišgāgâ) requires a whole burnt offering bull plus a sin offering male goat plus accompanying grain and drink offerings. The communal sin offering is more elaborate than the individual one (v27), reflecting the greater weight of communal culpability. The assembly (kol-hāʿēḏâ) must offer as a body; the sin is not distributable to individuals when it is a corporate failure of understanding.
25: wəkippar hakkōhēn ʿal-kol-ʿăḏat bənê yiśrāʾēl wənislaḥ lāhem — the priest shall make atonement for the whole congregation and they shall be forgiven. The atonement/forgiveness sequence here uses the passive nislāḥ — it was forgiven — with YHWH as implied agent, parallel to the sālaḥ of Numbers 14:20. Atonement is priestly work; forgiveness is divine action. The two are sequential and inseparable in the Levitical system.
26: The inclusivity of community forgiveness: the whole congregation plus the resident alien (wegam-laggēr) dwelling among them are forgiven, since the whole people was involved in the inadvertent sin. The legal inclusion of the gēr in community atonement parallels the inclusion in community offering (vv14-16). The covenant community's ritual acts cover those who have joined themselves to it, regardless of ethnic origin.
27: Individual unintentional sin requires a yearling female goat (ʿēz bat-šənātāh) as a sin offering. The diminished scale — one animal versus the communal bull-plus-goat — reflects individual versus corporate accountability. The sin offering for an individual corresponds to the lowest-cost sacrifice for the most common kind of inadvertent transgression, ensuring access for all economic strata.
28: The priest makes atonement for the individual who sinned unintentionally before YHWH, and forgiveness follows. The legal sequence — sin → offering → priestly atonement → divine forgiveness — is identical for individual and community. The consistency of the structure across different sin-scales establishes the universality of the atonement mechanism: the same theological logic applies regardless of the scope of the failure.
29: tôrâ ʾaḥat yihyeh lākem — one law for you — again the equalizing formula (cf. v16). Both native-born and resident alien come under the same inadvertent-sin law. The legal universality of the system is reinforced: one offering structure, one atonement process, one forgiveness outcome, for all within the covenant community regardless of origin.
30: The deliberate sinner: wəhannep̱eš ʾăšer-taʿăśeh bəyāḏ rāmâ — the person who acts with a high hand. בְּיָד רָמָה (bəyāḏ rāmâ) — with a raised/high hand — is the idiom for bold, presumptuous, defiant action; the same phrase describes Israel leaving Egypt (Exodus 14:8; Numbers 33:3) with triumphant confidence. Here it describes deliberate defiance of YHWH. Such a person ʾet-YHWH hûʾ megaddēp̱ — blasphemes YHWH — using the Piel of גָּדַף (gāḏap̱), to revile/insult. No atoning sacrifice exists for this category of sin.
31: The deliberate blasphemer has despised the word of YHWH (deḇar-YHWH bāzâ) and broken his commandment (miṣwātô hēp̱ar). The sentence: hikkārēt tikkārēt hann ep̱eš hahîʾ — that person shall utterly be cut off — using the kārēt penalty (death/excommunication) that appears throughout the Torah for the most serious offenses. The repetition (hikkārēt tikkārēt) — the cognate infinitive absolute — intensifies the certainty and completeness of the cutting off. Her iniquity is upon her (ʿăwōnāh bāh) — she bears it herself with no priestly transfer.
32: The case of the Sabbath-wood-gatherer (vv32-36) provides a concrete application of the high-handed sin principle. A man found gathering wood on the Sabbath day (bəyôm haššabbāt) is the canonical test case. Wood-gathering involves intentional, visible labor; the prohibition on Sabbath work was established (Exodus 20:8-11; Numbers 31:14-15). This is not inadvertent error but deliberate action in a clearly prohibited category.
33: The man is brought before Moses, Aaron, and the whole congregation — the full judicial structure of the covenant community. The legal process is public and communal, not private or informal. The presentation to all three — prophet, priest, and community — reflects the three-fold accountability structure of Israelite governance.
34: wayannîḥû ʾōtô bammišmār — they put him in custody. The reason: lōʾ p̱ōraš mah-yēʿāśeh lô — it had not been made clear what should be done to him. This is one of the rare cases where the Torah explicitly acknowledges a gap in the existing legislation at the time it was being established. The custody pending divine clarification shows the process by which case law expanded the Mosaic corpus (cf. Numbers 9:8; Numbers 27:5).
35: YHWH's direct answer: môt yûmat hāʾîš — the man shall be put to death. The double verb form (môt yûmat — die he shall die) is the standard Torah formula for capital offenses. The prescribed method: stoning by the whole congregation outside the camp. The communal execution — not by authority figures but by all the assembly — involves the community itself in the enforcement of covenant law.
36: The execution is carried out exactly as commanded: kəʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe — as YHWH commanded Moses. The act of execution is thus not mob violence but covenantal obedience. The case serves as a legal precedent establishing that the high-handed sin principle (v30-31) applies to Sabbath violation, and that the penalty is capital. The placement immediately after the šegāgâ/bəyāḏ rāmâ distinction makes the narrative case for the legislative principle.
37: A final new speech unit (vv37-41): the tassel law. The address formula again marks this as a discrete legal unit. The tassel law follows the sin-distinction laws, providing a positive mnemonic device as the practical solution to the problem the chapter has been addressing: how does Israel avoid falling into inadvertent or deliberate sin? By having constant visual reminders of the commandments.
38: ṣîṣit ʿal-kanp̱ê bigdêhem — tassels on the corners of their garments. צִיצִת (ṣîṣit) — tassel/fringe — appears only here and in Num 15 and Deuteronomy 22:12. A thread of תְּכֵלֶת (tékelet) — blue/violet dye — is to be woven into the tassel. Tékelet is the expensive marine-derived blue used in the tabernacle curtains (Exodus 26:1) and high priestly garments (Exodus 28:5,6). Including this color threads ordinary clothing into the symbolic world of the sanctuary.
39: ûrəʾîtem ʾōtô ûzəkartem ʾet-kol-miṣwōt YHWH — you shall look at them and remember all the commandments of YHWH. The tassel is a visual mnemonic (zikārôn-function): seeing triggers remembering. The chain of visual reminder → memory → action prevents the drift toward personal desire (ʾaḥărê lěbaḇkem) and eyes (ʾaḥărê ʿênêkem) that leads to spiritual infidelity. The phrase zōnîm ʾaḥărêhem — playing the harlot after them — uses the sexual-unfaithfulness metaphor for covenant violation.
40: lemaʿan tizkorû waʿăśîtem ʾet-kol-miṣwōtāy — so that you may remember and do all my commandments. The purpose of the tassels is behavioral: not meditation but obedience. wihyîtem qəḏōšîm lēʾlōhêkem — you shall be holy to your God. The holiness formula (qədôšîm) connects to the Levitical holiness code (Lev 19-20) and frames practical mnemonic technology within the theological category of covenant consecration.
41: ʾănî YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem ʾăšer hôṣêtî ʾetkem mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim lihyôt lākem lēʾlōhîm — I am YHWH your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God. The ʾănî YHWH formula — I am YHWH — appears throughout Leviticus as the grounding of covenantal obligation. The exodus event is cited as the constitutive act: being God to Israel is defined by the act of liberation. The chapter closes by returning to the foundational covenant relationship after its long legislative development.
